title: Lesson 235
transcript: The BIA process, business impact analysis. Ultimately, the BIA process is about identifying the most critical systems and processes within a business, determining how long those business processes and systems can be down before, the company is in serious trouble or out of business, and then determining what resources would be required to get those systems back up in a timely manner. So let's walk through how the BIA process actually works. What are the steps that are involved and what's involved in each of those steps? The BIA process can be a very long and arduous process, especially in a large business. It can sometimes take months to get through this process. Because the first step that's involved is identifying all the key stakeholders, all the owners out there that own business processes and different systems. And so the way the business, the BIA process typically starts out is by sending out surveys, asking to all parts of the business, what are your most critical systems and what are your most critical processes? And it's very much a data gathering exercise. And once those surveys come back, then there's a big validation step. Someone has to go through all of those surveys and see what did we miss? What else is out there? And so this typically takes a couple of rounds of iteration before all of the kind of most critical systems are out there. The next step is to ask all of those business owners, system owners, How critical is your business process? How critical is your system? And inevitably, every single one of them is going to say, wow, mine's the most critical process. Mine's the most critical system. I mean, what business owner is going to say, you know what? Yeah, what I do here, not that important. Honestly, I'm not even sure why I'm here. I mean, no one is going to say that. So everyone is going to say mine is the most critical process, which is challenging because not everything is the most critical process. So then the next step is typically some workshops. You get all the business owners in a room around the table. and you get them to argue, and you get them to figure out who truly has the most critical processes, which truly need to be up immediately, and which can actually be down for a period of time. And so a big part of this whole information gathering process is to determine how long these systems can be down for and how quickly do they need to be brought up. The next step in the BIA process is then to do a bunch of work. where you have to figure out, okay, you've told me it can only be down for this amount of time. Here's what it's gonna take to bring it back up in this amount of time, and here's especially what it's gonna cost. And in most cases, it's probably gonna cost a lot of money. So then one of the final steps of the BIA process is to go back to the business owners and say, okay, you told me your system can only be down for an hour. Here's what's gonna be required to make that happen. Here's all the architectural changes we have to make. Here's all the new systems we have to have online mirroring, replication, et cetera. And here's the cost of all that. And inevitably, what'll happen at this stage is the business owners will go, well, you want that much money? It's not that important. And so there's multiple rounds of iterations you have to go through before you finally arrive at, here's our most critical systems, here's how long they can be down for, and here's what we're gonna do to bring them back up. And it's out of this BIA process that we define these four critical measurements of time that we've been talking about. RPO, RTO, WRT, and MTD. We're gonna come up with these measurements of time for all these different systems. so that the final part of the BIA process is that we can then take these numbers, WRT, MTD, etc., and we can start to create the DRP and BCP plans.
